prompt: Quella sera ____ a lungo con mia madre. (parlare, io)
explanation: 'Quella sera parlai a lungo con mia madre.' (That evening I spoke at length with my mother.) When you tell of a single completed event finished in the past, the simple past you reach for in writing is the passato remoto, the remote past. A regular -are verb takes -ai in the io form: parlare gives parlai. 'Quella sera' fixes a finished past, so the present parlo and the imperfect parlavo (a habit or ongoing state) are both wrong here.

prompt: Quella sera Marco ____ a lungo davanti a tutti. (parlare)
explanation: 'Quella sera Marco parlò a lungo davanti a tutti.' (That evening Marco spoke at length in front of everyone.) The lui/lei form of a regular -are passato remoto ends in a stressed -ò, and the written accent is obligatory: parlare gives parlò. The accent is not decoration. It marks where the stress falls (par-LÒ, not PAR-lo) and it is the only thing on the page that separates this remote past from parlo, the io present of the same verb. Marco could not take parlo anyway (his present would be parla), so the accent here is carrying the tense, not resolving a clash of persons.

prompt: Quell'estate noi ____ benissimo ogni sera. (mangiare)
explanation: 'Quell'estate noi mangiammo benissimo ogni sera.' (That summer we ate very well every evening.) The noi form of the passato remoto doubles the m: -ammo. mangiare gives mangiammo. The single-m mangiamo is the present, and mangiavamo is the imperfect; 'quell'estate ... benissimo' frames a single remembered occasion, so the remote past is wanted.

prompt: I soldati ____ all'alba e occuparono la città. (arrivare)
explanation: 'I soldati arrivarono all'alba e occuparono la città.' (The soldiers arrived at dawn and occupied the city.) The loro form of a regular -are passato remoto ends in -arono: arrivare gives arrivarono. The clipped arrivarno is a frequent spoken slip; the present arrivano is the wrong tense. The companion verb occuparono shows the same pattern, anchoring the past.

prompt: Non gli ____ affatto, quella volta. (credere, io)
explanation: 'Non gli credei affatto, quella volta.' (I did not believe him at all, that time.) Regular -ere verbs accept two standard io endings in the passato remoto: -ei or -etti. credere gives credei or credetti, and neither is preferred over the other. Watch the spelling: the -ire double-i form credii is wrong, and credetti has a single d.
examiner_note: Dual -ei/-etti: both accepted at full credit per the leaf description. Diagnostic miss is borrowing the -ire pattern (credii).

prompt: Nessuno ci ____, all'inizio. (credere)

markpoints.label: 3sg credé / credette (dual; credé carries the accent)

must_not_include.note: crede is the present, 'he believes' - and it fits this sentence exactly as well as credé does, so only the accent tells the two apart. That is why it is a real wrong answer here, not a slip.

explanation: 'Nessuno ci credette, all'inizio.' (No one believed us, at first.) The lui/lei form of a regular -ere passato remoto is also dual: credé (with an obligatory acute accent) or credette. The bare crede is the present, and the -ire accent credì belongs to verbs like dormì, not to credere.

prompt: Quell'anno ____ la vecchia casa di famiglia. (vendere, io)
explanation: 'Quell'anno vendei la vecchia casa di famiglia.' (That year I sold the old family house.) Another regular -ere verb with the dual io ending: vendere gives vendei or vendetti. Both are fully standard. 'Quell'anno' marks a single completed sale, the remote past.

prompt: I soldati non ____ il pericolo e avanzarono. (temere)
explanation: 'I soldati non temerono il pericolo e avanzarono.' (The soldiers did not fear the danger and advanced.) The loro form is dual as well: temerono or temettero. The clipped temerno and the present-shaped temettono are wrong, and temevano is the imperfect. The companion avanzarono pins the remote past.

prompt: Quella notte non ____ affatto. (dormire, io)
explanation: 'Quella notte non dormii affatto.' (That night I did not sleep at all.) A regular -ire verb takes -ii in the io passato remoto, giving a written double i: dormire gives dormii. The single-i dormi is the present (or an imperative), the accented dormì is the lui/lei form, and dormivo is the imperfect. 'Quella notte ... non affatto' frames one finished night.

prompt: Il bambino ____ profondamente fino al mattino. (dormire)
explanation: 'Il bambino dormì profondamente fino al mattino.' (The child slept deeply until morning.) The lui/lei form of an -ire passato remoto ends in a stressed -ì with an obligatory written accent: dormire gives dormì. The accent marks the stress (dor-MÌ, not DOR-mi) and separates the remote past from dormi, the tu present. The child's own present would be dorme, so nothing but the accent is doing the tense work here. The imperfect dormiva would describe the sleeping rather than state it.

prompt: Solo allora noi ____ la verità. (capire)
explanation: 'Solo allora noi capimmo la verità.' (Only then did we understand the truth.) The passato remoto has no -isc- infix: capire builds straight on the stem, so the noi form is capimmo, never capiscemmo. The doubled m marks the remote past against the present capiamo; capivamo is the imperfect.
examiner_note: Left visible (not suppressed): a 'Regular -ire' breadcrumb tempts the catchable -isc- over-extension capiscemmo, which must_not_include catches. Productive-trap case per criterion 15.

prompt: Quella mattina ____ il primo treno per Roma. (prendere, io)
explanation: 'Quella mattina presi il primo treno per Roma.' (That morning I took the first train to Rome.) A large group of -ere verbs has an irregular 'strong' stem that shows up in just three persons (io, lui/lei, loro), with the io ending -i landing straight on it: prendere gives presi. Treat it as regular (prendei, prendetti) and you have missed the strong stem that is the whole point.
examiner_note: Strong-class membership is not derivable from the -ere infinitive, so suppressed per criterion 15.

prompt: Quella mattina tu ____ il primo treno per Roma. (prendere)
explanation: 'Quella mattina tu prendesti il primo treno per Roma.' (That morning you took the first train to Rome.) The strong stem appears in only three persons. The tu form is perfectly regular and built on the ordinary stem: prendere gives prendesti, not presesti. Carrying the strong stem into the middle persons (presesti) is the signature error.
examiner_note: Left visible: a 'strong 1-3-3' breadcrumb tempts presesti, the catchable over-extension caught by must_not_include. Half the diagnostic of this leaf is the regular middle person.

prompt: I ragazzi ____ l'ultimo autobus della sera. (prendere)
explanation: 'I ragazzi presero l'ultimo autobus della sera.' (The boys took the last bus of the evening.) The loro form sits on the strong stem with the ending -ero: prendere gives presero. The regularised prenderono / prendettero ignore the strong stem.

prompt: Quella notte ____ una lunga lettera. (scrivere, io)
explanation: 'Quella notte scrissi una lunga lettera.' (That night I wrote a long letter.) scrivere has the strong stem scriss-: the io form is scrissi, with a double s. The regularised scrivei/scrivetti miss the strong stem, and the single-s scrisi mis-spells it.

prompt: Manzoni ____ il suo romanzo nell'Ottocento. (scrivere)
explanation: 'Manzoni scrisse il suo romanzo nell'Ottocento.' (Manzoni wrote his novel in the nineteenth century.) The lui/lei form is scrisse, strong stem scriss- plus -e. Manzoni and the historical frame pin the remote past, the tense of literary and biographical narrative.

prompt: Quel giorno la ____ per la prima volta. (vedere, io)
explanation: 'Quel giorno la vidi per la prima volta.' (That day I saw her for the first time.) vedere has the strong stem vid-: the io form is vidi. The regularised vedei/vedetti and the doubled viddi are all wrong.

prompt: Tutti ____ la cometa quella notte. (vedere)
explanation: 'Tutti videro la cometa quella notte.' (Everyone saw the comet that night.) The loro form is videro, strong stem vid- plus -ero. Note the single d against the tempting viddero.

prompt: Lei ____ il libro sul tavolo e uscì. (mettere)
explanation: 'Lei mise il libro sul tavolo e uscì.' (She put the book on the table and went out.) mettere has the strong stem mis-: the lui/lei form is mise, with a single s. The regularised mettè/mettette and the double-s messe (the participle messo's relative) are wrong.

prompt: Quella sera ____ tutto in ordine prima di partire. (mettere, noi)
explanation: 'Quella sera mettemmo tutto in ordine prima di partire.' (That evening we put everything in order before leaving.) The noi form is regular, on the ordinary stem: mettere gives mettemmo, not misemmo. The strong stem mis- stays out of the middle persons.
examiner_note: Left visible: the breadcrumb tempts the catchable misemmo (strong stem in a middle person).

prompt: Allora le ____ scusa per tutto. (chiedere, io)
explanation: 'Allora le chiesi scusa per tutto.' (Then I apologised to her for everything.) chiedere has the strong stem chies-: the io form is chiesi. Do not confuse it with the past participle chiesto or write chiesti.

prompt: Il professore ____ la poesia ad alta voce. (leggere)
explanation: 'Il professore lesse la poesia ad alta voce.' (The professor read the poem aloud.) leggere has the strong stem less-: the lui/lei form is lesse, with a double s and a hard-to-soft change from gg to ss. The regularised leggé/leggette miss the strong stem.

prompt: I miei nonni ____ in campagna per molti anni. (vivere)
explanation: 'I miei nonni vissero in campagna per molti anni.' (My grandparents lived in the country for many years.) vivere has the strong stem viss-: the loro form is vissero. The regularised viverono/vivettero and the mis-formed vissono are all wrong.

prompt: Quel pomeriggio ____ a trovarci all'improvviso. (venire)
explanation: 'Quel pomeriggio venne a trovarci all'improvviso.' (That afternoon he came to visit us unexpectedly.) venire is an -ire verb that follows the strong pattern: the strong stem venn- gives the lui/lei form venne, with a double n. The accented venì would be the regular -ire shape, which venire does not take; veniva is the imperfect.

prompt: Voi quando ____ a Roma per la prima volta? (venire)
explanation: 'Voi quando veniste a Roma per la prima volta?' (When did you all come to Rome for the first time?) Even venire keeps the middle persons regular: the voi form is veniste, on the ordinary stem, not venneste. The strong stem venn- stays in the io/lui/loro forms only.
examiner_note: Left visible: tempts the catchable venneste. Voi is supplied, so the cue names the verb only (criterion 13 cue economy).

prompt: Quell'anno ____ davvero felice. (essere, io)
explanation: 'Quell'anno fui davvero felice.' (That year I was really happy.) essere is wholly suppletive in the passato remoto, built on an unrelated stem: fui, fosti, fu, fummo, foste, furono. The io form is fui. There is no regular essei.

prompt: Il viaggio ____ lungo e difficile. (essere)
explanation: 'Il viaggio fu lungo e difficile.' (The journey was long and difficult.) The lui/lei form is fu, with no accent. fu is one of the commonest words in Italian narrative. The imperfect era describes an ongoing state; fu states the event as a whole.

prompt: Quelli ____ anni molto difficili. (essere)
explanation: 'Quelli furono anni molto difficili.' (Those were very difficult years.) The loro form is furono. The clipped furno is a common spoken reduction; the imperfect erano frames a background state rather than the bounded stretch the remote past gives.

prompt: In quel momento ____ una gran paura. (avere, io)
explanation: 'In quel momento ebbi una gran paura.' (At that moment I was very afraid.) avere follows the strong 1-3-3 pattern on the stem ebb-: ebbi, avesti, ebbe, avemmo, aveste, ebbero. The io form is ebbi, with a double b. The regularised avei/avetti and the single-b ebi are wrong. ebbi is also one auxiliary of the trapassato remoto.

prompt: Allora ____ un'idea improvvisa. (avere)
explanation: 'Allora ebbe un'idea improvvisa.' (Then he had a sudden idea.) The lui/lei form is ebbe, on the strong stem ebb-. ebbe is extremely frequent in narrative ('ebbe paura', 'ebbe un figlio'). The regularised avé/avette miss the strong stem.

prompt: Tu ____ ragione fin dall'inizio. (avere)
explanation: 'Tu avesti ragione fin dall'inizio.' (You were right from the very start.) avere keeps its middle persons regular, on the stem av-: the tu form is avesti, not ebbesti. The strong stem ebb- appears in the io/lui/loro forms only; avevi is the imperfect.
examiner_note: Left visible: tempts the catchable ebbesti (strong stem in a middle person).

prompt: Quel giorno ____ una scelta difficile. (fare, io)
explanation: 'Quel giorno feci una scelta difficile.' (That day I made a difficult choice.) fare has the strong stem fec-: feci, facesti, fece, facemmo, faceste, fecero. The io form is feci. The regularised facei/facetti and the clipped fei are wrong.

prompt: Leonardo ____ molti disegni e studi. (fare)
explanation: 'Leonardo fece molti disegni e studi.' (Leonardo made many drawings and studies.) The lui/lei form is fece, on the strong stem fec-. It is very common in biographical narrative. The middle persons keep the regular stem fac- (facesti, facemmo, faceste).

prompt: Mi ____ la mano e se ne andò. (dare)
explanation: 'Mi diede la mano e se ne andò.' (He gave me his hand and went away.) dare has two accepted forms in the strong persons: diede or dette in the lui/lei (diedi/detti in the io). Both are standard. Do not confuse with the present dà or the imperfect dava.
examiner_note: Dual diede/dette flagged for the project author in the coverage report; both accepted at full credit.

prompt: Quella sera ____ a casa da solo. (stare, io)
explanation: 'Quella sera stetti a casa da solo.' (That evening I stayed at home alone.) stare has the strong stem stett-: stetti, stesti, stette, stemmo, steste, stettero. The io form is stetti, with a double t. The regularised stei and the imperfect stavo are wrong.

prompt: Non ____ una sola parola in tutta la sera. (dire)
explanation: 'Non disse una sola parola in tutta la sera.' (He did not say a single word all evening.) dire has the strong stem diss-: dissi, dicesti, disse, dicemmo, diceste, dissero. The lui/lei form is disse, with a double s. The middle persons keep the regular stem dic- (dicesti, dicemmo).

prompt: Appena ____ di parlare, tutti applaudirono. (finire)
explanation: 'Appena ebbe finito di parlare, tutti applaudirono.' (As soon as he had finished speaking, everyone applauded.) The trapassato remoto sets one completed action just before another in a narrative. It pairs the passato remoto of the auxiliary with the past participle: here the avere form ebbe plus finito gives 'ebbe finito'. 'Appena ... applaudirono' (itself a remote past) is the exact frame this rare, literary tense lives in. Use the pluperfect aveva finito and you have the everyday past, not the remote one.
examiner_note: Two markpoints per criterion 8: the passato remoto auxiliary (this tree) and the participle (PP tree). The pluperfect aveva is the diagnostic miss; the simple passato remoto finì misses the auxiliary markpoint.

prompt: Dopo che ____ partito, ci pentimmo della lite. (partire)
explanation: 'Dopo che fu partito, ci pentimmo della lite.' (After he had left, we regretted the quarrel.) partire is a verb of motion, so its trapassato remoto takes essere, not avere: the passato remoto essere form fu plus the participle partito gives 'fu partito'. The participle agrees with the masculine singular subject. 'Dopo che ... pentimmo' frames the prior completed departure. The pluperfect era partito would be the everyday past.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: essere passato remoto auxiliary (this tree) and the participle, masculine-singular agreement. A feminine subject would need partita.

prompt: Quando ____ le valigie, uscirono di casa. (fare)
explanation: 'Quando ebbero fatto le valigie, uscirono di casa.' (When they had packed the suitcases, they left the house.) The loro auxiliary in the passato remoto is ebbero, and fare's irregular participle is fatto, giving the trapassato remoto 'ebbero fatto'. 'Quando ... uscirono' sets it just before the main remote-past event. The pluperfect avevano fatto is the ordinary past, not this literary one.
examiner_note: Two markpoints: avere passato remoto auxiliary 3pl (this tree) and fare's irregular participle (PP irregular tree).
